does: This paper introduces the Triangulated Preference Shift (TPS) score, an automated metric designed to identify and measure lexical biases introduced specifically during preference learning stages like RLHF. The metric works by triangulating between three sources: human gold standards, base model outputs, and instruction-tuned variants, allowing it to isolate preference-induced shifts without requiring manual curation of test data. The authors apply this metric across six model families to quantify systematic patterns such as overuse of particular words and stylistic conventions that emerge after preference training, providing empirical evidence of how instruction tuning and RLHF shape model output distributions in ways that diverge from natural language.
connects: This work builds on growing recognition that preference learning methods, while improving model usefulness and alignment, introduce unintended systematic biases in model behavior. It extends prior research on model misalignment and stylistic artifacts by providing a quantitative, scalable alternative to manual curation-based analysis. The research connects to literature on bias measurement in NLP, post-training effects on language models, and the broader study of how RLHF shapes model behavior beyond its intended objectives. It responds to limitations in existing evaluation approaches that either rely on labor-intensive manual annotation or fail to distinguish preference-induced shifts from base model characteristics.
enables: This automated triangulation metric enables systematic auditing of preference learning effects across model families and training approaches at scale. It could support development of preference learning methods that reduce unwanted lexical bias while maintaining alignment benefits, allowing researchers to iteratively test interventions and measure their success. The work points toward more rigorous characterization of what preferences learning actually optimizes for beyond standard benchmarks, potentially informing better training procedures that align models without introducing spurious stylistic artifacts. The metric framework could extend beyond lexical analysis to other dimensions of model behavior, enabling broader investigation of preference learning's side effects.
